channel.title: +मानसतरङ्गिणीकृल्-लेखाः on Vishvas's notes
channel.description: Recent content in +मानसतरङ्गिणीकृल्-लेखाः on Vishvas's notes

item.title: Sultans felled
item.description: Source: here. A poet rAmarAja-bhuShaNa in the Vijayanagara court composed this generous account for the Moslem tyrants who met their end at kR^ishNadeva rAya’s hand: “O kR^iShNa rAya, the foremost of heroes! Some proud and mighty Moslem kings killed by you in battle have arrived in svarga [1]. Here they caused the devas to smile in amusement by their salaaming and addressing bR^ihaspati as pIr, indra as suratrANa, and indrANi as bIbI.

item.title: The location of Vijayanagara
item.description: Source: here. The location of Vijayanagara That great capital of the Hindu revival- remarkably placed on the mid-stream of the life-giving Tungabhadra.

item.title: The post-Talikota struggle
item.description: Source: here. After the fall of Vijayanagar some historians believe that Tirumala, the brother of Ramaraya made an attempt to revive the city but failed. It is believed that there was a conflict between him and his nephew Pedda Tirumala over the control of the city and hence he decided to abandon it.+++(4)+++ With the Vijayanagar army in shambles, Ramaraya’s nepotism at the expense of traditional Hindu civil officers left the whole kingdom in a state of chaos.

item.title: The reign of Ramaraya and the battle of Talikota.
item.description: Source: here. One of the most fateful events in Hindu history was the battle of Talikota that occurred during the time of Ramaraya who was the de facto ruler of Vijayanagar. Several attempts have been made by secularist writers to paint Ramaraya as a villain and downplay his eventful reign. He did commit miscalculations in different directions: 1) He killed many of Hindu Nayakas and replaced them by his own clansmen resulting in considerable discontent amongst the Hindu elite.
